replies.author: @free100x
replies.text:
The sodium graphene reaction has been known for a year or more and is very cool. Houses, cars, roads, and more might be coated to generate energy when it rained or snowed. Then coating the inside of water pipes and water heaters might be possible, to generate even more power.

But as Robert points out we don't know for sure until  the experiments are done. Graphene can perform many functions/tricks all at the same time.

replies.author: @free100x
replies.text:

http://arstechnica.com/science/2014/04/flowing-salt-water-over-graphene-generates-electricity/

author: @ForreignEpicMealTime
text: Hey rob, i'm loving all this innovation. I've been following graphene for the past 3 years now and it fascinates me that it still just seams to be hobbyist and engineers messing around with most of the technology. I just can't wait for when governments and large corporations start to fully invest in these technologies so we can advance as a society. That said as a 19 year old who plays video games from time to time i can say you certainly don't have to give up video games to get involved in science. The streotype for 'nerds' says it all really. I think it's more that you have to work up the more complex and exciting parts of science by getting kids to wanting to learn more about the basics, by showing them the cool side of physics. e.g discharging a 3000F cap through a coin or setting fire to KNO3.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's garage science mate - it happened to music in the 80s and now science in the 2010s - and i agree with you on the point about games - it's when it becomes all about games that i think it is a bit of a mistake

author: @ErikCLehmann1
text: Wow, I love the conversation that has been started here, and Robert, thank you for posting this to your page!  I would like to add that the Game Changer Movement wishes to change the content of the games, while meeting gamers in the game space.  We are working towards embedding the properties of graphene into the game space, so that youth can be exposed to this amazing stuff, early and often.  We are then going to find ways to synthesize some of the more promising innovations that occur in the game space using files converted and rendered for 3D printing with Graphene composite filament!  Please continue to add your insights to the conversation!  There are billions of hours to redirect, and all of your input is tremendously valuable!  Thank you, once again!  All my best, Erik

author: @darkstalkerknight63
text:
questions. 

can you make a one inch to two inches thick full body armor with a clear face shield out of graphene, plastic and/or rubber.

how much would that weight in total for the average size person (male and female). 

how much would that weight in total for the average size car. can you make tires out of it.

how much would that weight in total for the average size house (then times two because I want to make put outside of wires, pipes, and spray foam insulation). also can you make pipes and wires out of it

can you make Solar panels and wind turbines out of it.

I think you know you have to do it in layers of graphene and plastic and/or rubber. how thin can you get the plastic or rubber to be able to get as many layers of graphene into that one inch to two inches thickness.

I think that's all

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - i guess you could mate but i am unlikely to and i have no idea what it would weigh in at

replies.author: @darkstalkerknight63
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
how many layers of graphene did you use in your bullet tests.

I am guessing only 1 but I want to be sure.

it was a lot of questions that came to my mind

if we do it by layers and layers of graphene and a 1mm layer of plastic and/or rubber to 1 inch think, we could get 25 layers of graphene . if you use only one layer of graphene in your bullet tests and it was enough to stop a ak-47 round then 25 layers could stop any bullet and 50 layers could take an RPG and 200 layers could take a bomb while being at least 10 times lighter than what we use now

if that is the case, police would not need guns anymore because no bullets would be able to get though their armors of they wear it right and we could make cars where people would not die in a crash if they were their car-belts and even if it landed in water it would not go under.

and it is too light for you to make bullets out of it as well.

author: @Alexandr22ru
text:
Thank you for subtitles on the video !!! So much is clear.
About children and science, my opinion. I myself liked to spend another week in the computer game. Sychas A father of two teenagers. And the Son of computer games is very addictive, like a disease, there were mental disorders and a bad score. Our way out - strict control of time of computer games and cooperative games and activities: go to stalion, play designer, who will learn the verse faster help for working in the garden, and much more. Maynkraft is not the most important thing in his life ....
Much more important that thanks to your video, Robert, I myself became interested and began to experiment. I had to relearn the course of inorganic chemistry, materials and tools to collect, make imicheskuyu mini laboratory, buy chemical reagents. It's all different quests that had to be solved. And of course along the way my kids have seen a lot of different experiments and tricks and is really excites. Here is my method.
With superkondesatorom graphene and I have not really poluchaetsya. Many reagents are not commercially available. But it is not as important as it is interesting. Now I have one more hobby.
Thank you, Robert.
Alexander from Russia.
PS Sorry for the English - Google

author: @WileHeCoyote
text:
The Future of Video games and all other forms of Media is here, Now! Computers, smart phones, Tablets,  "Virtual Reality" and "Augmented reality" .   All of which can now be purchased and will soon be everywhere. 

Virtual and Augmented reality devices are nothing more or less than the most immersive, engaging, and in depth learning tools we"ve ever created. 

 Rather than waste energy fighting the flow of a technologic generation, telling them they play to many video games and so on, lets instead support or help create the worlds most interactive and fun, learning video games ever. Then if they really like the game, they are instantly studying students of science, instead of being "kids wasting time" playing video games.

replies.author: @ErikCLehmann1
replies.text:
This is exactly what we are embarking on.  We are creating a collaborative process to co-create an interactive game that actually has real world impact i.e. FoldIt, EteRNA.  Want to collaborate?
All my best, Erik

replies.author: @WileHeCoyote
replies.text:
+Erik Lehmann (Game Changer Movement)  I would love to collaborate or help in any way that I can. I have always been an avid supporter of all   educational and science based video games. Math blasters was my one of my favorite games back in the day, and I was astounded how much better at math I was without even trying. lol. 

Education should be fun, engaging, and immersive, when ever possible.

author: @WileHeCoyote
text:
(On the subject of violent video games) There's good books and bad books, but just because there are some hateful or violent books, we would never discourage an avid reader from the activity of reading. 

Theres good video games and bad video games, lets encourage and create more educational video games, rather than argue over which information input form is better "reading on paper" "reading on a computer screen" "listening to audio books" "listening to radio news" "watching educational programming" "Interacting with educational programing" "playing educational games"  Semantics!! they all have a roll and they all synch with people differently or not at all.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: true enough mate

author: @WileHeCoyote
text: I think it is important to be careful, when we are generalizing "video games" as a whole, or when punishing children for enjoying an interactive media/information input form. How many of us now spend hours, every day, on smart phones, computer, news stations, books, magazines or news papers. They are all information sources to me, with only varying levels of interactivity.  I think parents and teachers should gently steer or guide games and content, and not expend too much energy prohibiting a certain media format.  I struggled through most of my public school education because i am just not wired for reading and righting as others are. Now that technology has advanced to a format that works best for me, i watch educational videos for hours, listen to audio books for hours! and i am able to recall far more than if i had read the information.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: personally i am a kinaesthetic learner so learn best by physically doing it

author: @Jantzen7

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @ErikCLehmann1
replies.text: There is a ton of research out there, that is not asking the right questions.  Instead of asking, "Do violent games make kids more violent or aggressive?"  They could ask, Do kids receive training that could translate into the real world, when real weapons are involved?  The answer to that question is yes!  This is what I call, the Imperfect Storm.  The military uses violent games as training modules, and even treatment for those experiencing PTSD from the killing fields.  The difference is, there are safeguards and consequences for trainees in the police force or military.  If someone shoots too much ammo, or too quickly, there are real life reprimands.  If that happens in the game world, someone gets respawned or more points.  That said, the main vision of the Game Changer Movement is to redirect the billions of hours spent, learning how to kill and investing those hours into innovation with things like Graphene!

replies.author: @Jantzen7
replies.text: Erik Lehmann Thats great and God bless and may Christ be with you for that! As a tip though, it is ok to talk about the video game stuff towards investors, parents, actual adult interviews etc. but when it comes to the actual execution of the project towards kids, don't focus on the video game part as it will just turn off their heads and will not want to listen instead focus on showing them how it involves in their lives relatably (ie kid wants a motorcycle but too young to get one so show him how to make a motorized bicyle instead)

author: @sss-tw3jh
text: Video games are pass time tings, like movies or a favorite tv show. A good percentage of people in my age group, (basically anyone born 1995-1999) are pretty intelligent people. Although there are the bad eggs, the dumb ones, the kids who put gaming or social media at the center of their existence in some kind of chronic mental masturbation. But that's not saying they aren't Keen on things like working together to solve a problem, which is actually one of the fastest skills you develop through playing video games. Despite the bad eggs lack of desire to innovate, teambuilding and quick learning skills are already pre installed and optimized from the gamer's mind. Its not some kind of battle to eliminate gaming or suppress it. Its just making your field and science more appealing and simplistic on its surface. Maybe even a bit more comical. This is coming from a 16 year old college student pursuing computer science. I'm interested in silicon myself, but I recognize battery technology and just basic energy storage are vital for the future. Despite being an avid watcher of this channel, avidly interested in experimentation, and my studies, I still play a shitload of video games. When off hours and weekends come, its time to relax, kick back, and wreck some people.

author: @phano6385
text:
I want to say that if I was not exposed to video games, I don't know if I would have taken an interest in electronics engineering or computer science. Maybe I would have; maybe I'm just inquisitive by nature... 
You yourself have said we are largely a product of our environment. Of course, when I was a kid, computers were a little bit easier to get inside, and indeed, I needed to be fluent in command line programs to get any game to run. Now it is as simple as pushing a button. (if even that!) So I'm not disagreeing with you on any particular point.

I'm working very hard to create my own games which I see as a combination of many skills and artistic talents. I doubt my own skills in making something directly educational while still being fun, but there is always a chance for an indirect exposure to certain concepts and sciences.

I have already decided to be as open and explanatory in my techniques and insights behind the crafting of these games. I want people to learn how these magical interactive  ... Things work. Perhaps I will put even a little more effort to educate, to help everyone learn something new.

But of course, it all depends on the will of the individual!

Thank you for your time and consideration.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Mate! - i think what you said was spot on. Open up the skills not the recipe. That way people learn - inmy mind all things have equal weight - i tend to be science oriented but that is only my bent - music, art, codeing are all explorations in the way i see it and therefore all equal - it doesn't have to be science but it does have to be creative and teaching the art of creation and creativity is where it is all at - everything is a vehicle for that

author: @randytravis5893
text: I fear you wont get many kids off games, just by trying with encouragement. What would work would be to get intelligent kids away from games and into the real world, is to be sneaky and use games to draw them out to real possibilities in the real world. Games like mine craft and survival crafting games can use real concepts of science and mathematics to create things in games, but also expose what can be done in the real world. There is a computer game company called steam, they make games and players can buy the soft ware to write their own games. If some one with talent could pump out some survival games that involved real chemistry, graphene could be included, and I'm sure you would get lots of kids flocking to real science. I suggest mine craft as well, it would make a great simulator for real science, and you can even have separate adventures for different subjects. I wish I could write programs, I would do it myself. Millions of kids play minecraft and steam games. If you ever have too much money and want to spend some to help kids, my advice would be to hire some good game programmers, in these areas and make some fun educational games that involve survival and challenges. Its simple really. Kids love loot!! treasure!! just make it impossible to get to good loot with out learning to make the materials, and in order to make the materials you have to have real knowledge of chemistry, math, ect...Trust me, I know, I was a game junke for years! Kids will put forth great effort just to get bragging rights on getting a level 100 so and so with , lets say a graphene energy pack, and learn all about how to do it by doing simulations  and calulations over and over, pluss all the math and research involved just to accomplish a game challenge. A game called borderlands had kids putting over 50 hours a week playing for months just to get special weapons. I bet if some one with your caliber of reputation were to put forth the suggestion there might be some government funding avalible, Many millions if not billions of dollars have been given for stupid projects that had no effect on helping kids, why not try to effect kids where they really put there attention? Games could be the next big educational jump forward, if they are created with entertainment in mind. I know kids waste their time playing games, even tho they hate parts of it. They call it grinding! Repetitive actions in gamming that you need to do to aquire materials and supplies, do puzzles ect...  I don't see why they wouldn't do the same stuff, but use real chemistry and math.I know several people who make games for minecraft, and I can find you the best people who make steam games. It doesn't take much money for those types of gamming platforms, and mabe none at all if you can get some government interest involved. Hell there are many collage studdents who do it for fun and just getting funding for a grant for collage would get you tons of willing programmers and there are plenty of companies willing to give out money for collage, its tax deductible.well, hope you like my idea, I have been trying to spin it for years, but I'm a nobuddy so no one really cares what I say, they all tell me its a great idea, but they already got stuff going on. Trust me, you want kids to stop playing pointless games, then make games that are not so pointless, because I don't see any way to stop the game craze. A good example of games becoming useful are drones. Flying drones with cameras and visors, its just like your flying in real life, like a bird.If you like the Idea, I can find you relivent people, but unless some one like you is willing to be involved its not going any where. I'm not saying you have to drop every thing, just mention this idea to important people who ask you about what you think would be a great thing that would help kids. A collage picking up this concept, as a research in alternative  education could change the world.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nicely put mate

replies.author: @randytravis5893
replies.text:
Thanks, I know you are very occupied, but its amazing what a person with respect and caliber like you, can influence people. I know, at some point, important people will seek your guidance, just like they did Einenstine. I think this approach of reaching young minds with games is the best way, but you need to make sure that real gamers and programmers make the game, and make it fun and challenging the way kids like it. Professors and acidemics can ensure the correct information is convayed in the game, but the teachers usually destroy the fun if they try and direct how the game should be made.
Thanks for listening, It really makes me happy to know some of my ideas have some interest value.
Right now I'm trying to make a diy cordless soldering iron, its not going too well.
Randy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are 100% right mate - there is nothing worse than an academic trying to be 'cool' lol - keep at it mate - that's the only real key

text:
Love it!  hhmmm just thinking a-loud here.  I want to link you Robert, Erik & things like experimentation processes, especially the ones that require kids to MAKE the equipment used. The page I'm just getting started on is silohousefarm.com you can look around on it now as I'm putting it together. I'm not sure how to tie Science in with Homesteading, Farming yet. But I know it's a great fit!  If anybody has some ideas please let me know.  
Peace Out!

author: @strongforce8466
text:
Don't really have time to check this more in depth  but I like ideas that promote teaching, especially new ways of teaching, the old style is outdated in my opinion, videos are the way to teach nowadays, now I'm not saying we don't need teachers anymore but we should take advantage of videos more in teaching, especially since it's more entertaining, or can be designed that way at least. 

I have been a gamer since more than 15  years, I think, as long as it's not too violent, and not promoting an agenda, it's good! also, just not pushing violences and bad things like that to kids.. that goes without saying, problem is just about 90% of the games out there involve some kind of violence.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am the same mate - i tend to support anything that involves teaching and i always admire someone who will get up and try.

replies.author: @strongforce8466
replies.text: yea and new form of teaching such as interactive environment are certainly a path to look forward to, yup agree, even if I don't understand or fully agreen 100% I still encourage him, anyway, violent games can't do any good to kids so..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah i am about the same mate - i don't 100% grasp what he's trying to do - but i do think he is trying to make a difference

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries mate - just for your info - we are building a large version to run a motor bike and we are doing it right now - it's probably slower than you want because we are testing every stage of it - we don't just throw things together - it's the scientist in me and i can appreciate the frustration your side - but if we just built one and it went wrong we wouldn't know where it went wrong and it would take a lot longer to fix - so we do it stage by stage - lol

replies.author: @dft9786
replies.text: It is not frustration, it is obvious it always takes longer in practice than in theory. Just wanted to know how it was going. You dont mention it much in the latest videos. Wish you luck anyway.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @theapexsurvivor9538
text:
I'm personally quite the gamer myself (even if I'm rather poor at it), but I still love science and discovery. In fact, I've found that they can fuel each other,  much like how the games we used to play as children in the school-yard lead to, and fueled our study of things like physics and biology.
I do agree that sometimes video games can be seem rather mindless and repetitive, and sometimes they don't seem to inspire much desire to learn, but the same could be said of any sport, yet sports can build teamwork, or help those who need practical examples of things like aerodynamics to learn.
For video games what we learn may not seem like much, yet in truth they can make pattern recognition second nature, can make it easier to identify things that you might normally ignore, drastically improve memory and creativity, morality (n.b. Not all that is moral is ethical), and much more.
Heck, it can even teach people the sciences and arts, through people learning to code, which can be useful in anything from engineering to genetics, people learning to work with electronics and machinery, people appreciating beautiful vistas or better yet creating them, which leads into fine art, learning to cook (which helps me keep my girlfriend happy and well fed), or even learning how we work, how our brains work, so that we can move into VR (specifically FullDive(see SAO NerveGear)) which is half of the reason I found your awesome channel (was studying the potential of using graphene for light-weight internal battery and for circuits (tin-monoxide would be more suited for circuits though (sorry way off topic))).
And though I do admit some games are worse than others (i.e. most mainstream shooters are horrible),  I think too often as a society we simply think of video games as one thing, such as mind-numbing AAA title shooters such as Salmon or War Oval, when in fact they are often vastly different, even if that means they have to be almost identical(and even then they are still unique, as no creator is quite alike).
Anyway, I just wanted to make sure that video games had some defense, I had when my two favourite hobbies fight, as science and gaming are both great ways to pass the time.
Ranting aside, I love your channel and your content, and hope you keep making more.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: post mate and an interesting read - personally i don't play games so i know next to nothing about them - but what you said certainly made me think of them differently and sparked a few ideas - thanks for the share

replies.author: @supercoe
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Please listen to this advice. As a long time gamer (32yo) it's sad to see people dislike games. I'm a self taught home schooler that played Doom every day instead of going to public school. I loved the game so much that I started making modifications for it which taught me programming and design. 

Thousands of people have been helped 
In my businesses because of what I learned playing games. 

You seem like a understanding guy Robert, please look at evidence before making claims like this. Video games are teaching tools more than you can imagine.  

You make me feel like you're the kind of guy that hates guns because all they do is kill. :-/ 

I enjoy your videos and opinions because they are usually well researched. This one however lacks. Maybe we could play some games together if you ever feel like researching it. :)

replies.author: @ron4937
replies.text: +supercoe I fear your case may be the exception rather than the rule. Most see video games as an escape and distraction from the dull life (which is either education or day job), and they are designed with a reward system in mind to get and keep people running on the hamster wheel and the money flowing. Not saying games could not be creative and practical, but the vast majority of them certainly isn't.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that sounds like an offer to take up mate - i have played a couple of games but they weren't for me and you are right in saying that just because i don't really play them doesn't mean they are necessarily 'bad' - i did look at the research on this but it is quite likely to be biased and you make me think i haven't looked into it enough. The main core of what i think is as long as a thing supports creativity it is a good thing - if it supports passive adsorption then it should be questioned - i may have been a bit hasty lol - sorry about that and i will revisit this

replies.author: @theapexsurvivor9538
replies.text: +Ron Garren I can't speak for super, but I do view gaming is an escape from school and work, but that doesn't mean that it isn't teaching me stuff, just means that the learning is less bland and disinteresting as that which school forces down my throat, and faster too, thanks to games I didn't need to pay attention in maths until year 8 as I had already covered the topics by year 3, which left me a free unit to study biochemistry 4.5 years earlier than school taught it.

author: @kennybentley1161
text: I wouldn't fight against video games specifically. I think that kids should do what they want because I believe that creativity comes from having time to think -- and there's no better time to think than when you're doing what you want. the kids that want to work on graphene or the millions of other things will happen naturally if the kid finds it interesting... I think they just need the opportunity - and then they'll go at it at the speed that they want. great message!!  thanks for sharing

author: @roidroid
text: Games are how modern kids absorb a lot of sci-fi ideas.  I worry that getting kids to stop playing games, is about as good an idea as discouraging kids from reading books.  It'd be separating them from a major source of inspiration :(

replies.author: @robmann9951
replies.text:
Empathising with this, i wandered over to Erik's site to read more. I found myself shifting from wanting to help to wanting to absolutely oppose! I agree video games can find a way to grab a lot of your time with many genre's, not just shooters. But to state that they are the cause of college shootings is scaremonger nonsense.

quote..
violent video game play has produced the legacies of Adam Linza, the shooter at Sandy Hook Elementary School, Christopher Harper-Mercer, the shooter at Umpqua Community College, and unfortunately countless other shooters who have learned how to kill; mostly from playing violent video games.

author: @BenCollinsDigital
text: Quite kind to post this for Erik, what a nice and cool person.  As a parent of a 15 year old hardcore gamer, I've had to ensure he still also reads profusely, gets straight A's in school, and continuously explores Wikipedia, YouTube, and the outdoors for knowledge. Also, since much of the time he lives far away, I can play games with him.  This summer I am going to work together with him on some graphene experiments in my makeshift lab!  I have some high grade graphene flakes to play with and some basic equipment.
